content.text: Chatbots and agents are both built on large language models, and the surface resemblance can obscure a fundamental architectural difference. Understanding where they diverge — and why it matters — is essential for choosing the right tool and for reasoning clearly about agentic systems.
What it is
A
chatbot

is a conversational interface backed by an LLM. The user sends a message, the LLM generates a reply, and the exchange is over. The chatbot may maintain a conversation history across turns, but it is fundamentally reactive: it waits for input, responds, and stops. It does not take actions in the world beyond producing text.
An
agent

is a goal-directed system that uses an LLM to decide what to do next, executes actions through tools, observes the results, and continues until the goal is achieved. The human provides a goal — not a series of messages — and the agent operates autonomously from there. It can search the web, write and run code, read files, call APIs, and chain these actions together without waiting for human approval at each step.
The core distinction is
action scope
. Chatbots produce text. Agents produce effects — they change state in external systems.
Why it matters

Confusing the two patterns leads to systems that are either under-powered or unnecessarily risky. A team that adds tool use to a chatbot without redesigning the interaction model ends up with an agent that the user tries to drive turn-by-turn — an awkward hybrid that is neither a good chat experience nor an effective autonomous system.

Conversely, wrapping a simple FAQ lookup in an agentic loop wastes compute, introduces non-determinism, and slows the user down for no benefit. Knowing which pattern fits the task determines the entire system design: the UI, the safety controls, the cost model, and the testing strategy.

The distinction also matters for user trust. People interact with chatbots and agents differently. A chatbot is a conversation partner — users expect it to be helpful but understand that nothing happens until they act on its output. An agent acts on their behalf — users must trust it with credentials, file access, or the ability to send emails. This higher trust bar requires correspondingly more robust safety design.
Benefits
Chatbot benefits
Low operational risk
: produces text only; cannot modify files, call APIs, or spend money.
Predictable cost
: one LLM call per turn, so cost per interaction is fixed and easy to budget.
Simple UX
: users understand the conversational paradigm intuitively.
Easy to audit
: every output is visible to the user before anything acts on it.
Fast iteration
: prompts are easy to tune; output quality is immediately visible.
Agent benefits
Autonomous task completion
: achieves multi-step goals without requiring the user to drive each step.
Real-world effects
: can search, write files, run code, call APIs — producing outcomes, not just descriptions of outcomes.
Self-correction
: when a tool call fails, the agent observes the error and tries an alternative — no human needed to unblock it.
Scales human effort
: one high-level instruction replaces minutes or hours of manual work.
Trade-offs

Chatbots trade capability for safety and simplicity. They cannot complete tasks that require external action; the user must do that work themselves after reading the chatbot's output. For many use cases — customer support, Q&A, drafting — this is exactly the right tradeoff.

Agents trade safety and predictability for capability. An agent that can send emails can also send the wrong email. An agent that can delete files can delete the wrong files. The same autonomy that makes agents powerful makes them risky if poorly designed. Agents also have variable cost and latency — a simple goal might take two steps, a complex one might take twenty.

Chatbots are also easier to evaluate: output quality is visible and measurable directly. Agent evaluation is harder — the agent may produce the right final result via an inefficient or unexpected path, or produce a wrong result that superficially looks right until inspected closely.
How it works
Chatbot execution model
A chatbot maintains a conversation history (a list of
{role, content}
pairs). On each user turn, it appends the new message, sends the full history to the LLM, and appends the response. The interaction is a simple request-response loop driven entirely by the user.

The LLM's output is always text (or structured data extracted from text). The chatbot does not use the LLM's output to trigger further actions — it displays the text and waits for the next user message. Every action that results from the conversation is taken by the human reading the output.
Agent execution model

An agent receives a goal and enters a loop. Each iteration: the LLM sees the accumulated context (goal + prior tool calls + prior results) and outputs either a tool call or a final answer. If it outputs a tool call, the runtime executes it and feeds the result back. If it outputs a final answer, the loop terminates.

The agent is not waiting for the user between loop iterations. It is executing autonomously, accumulating evidence from tool results, and deciding its own next step. The user set the goal at the start; the agent drives from there.
Architecture

The diagram below contrasts the two architectures: the chatbot's single-turn request-response loop driven by the user, versus the agent's autonomous goal-directed loop that uses tools and self-terminates when done.

attrs.alt: Chatbot vs Agent architecture: chatbot shows User↔LLM request-response loop; agent shows autonomous Goal→Perceive+Reason→Act→Tools→Observe loop

content.text: In the chatbot model, every loop iteration requires a human message. In the agent model, the loop runs autonomously until the LLM outputs a final answer — the human only appears at the start (goal) and end (result).
When to use it
Use a chatbot when
The task involves answering questions, explaining concepts, or helping the user draft text.
The user must review the output before any action is taken (information is the product).
The interaction requires back-and-forth clarification before a decision can be made.
Operational risk must be minimized — the system should not be able to take harmful actions automatically.
Cost per interaction must be tightly controlled.
Use an agent when
The task requires taking actions in external systems (files, APIs, browsers, databases).
The task has multiple steps that cannot all be determined in advance.
Human-in-the-loop for each step would be too slow or too tedious.
Self-correction is necessary — the system must recover from tool errors without human intervention.
Do not use an agent when
A single LLM call would suffice — an agentic loop adds latency, cost, and non-determinism for no benefit.
The user needs fine-grained control over each action — they will be frustrated by opacity.
Tool access would give the agent the ability to cause irreversible harm without adequate safeguards.
How to implement
Building a chatbot
A chatbot is a conversation history plus an LLM call on each turn. The implementation is minimal:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def run_chatbot():
    history = []
    system = "You are a helpful assistant. Answer questions clearly and concisely."

    while True:
        user_input = input("You: ").strip()
        if user_input.lower() in ("quit", "exit"):
            break

        history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_input})

        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            max_tokens=1024,
            system=system,
            messages=history
        )

        reply = response.content[0].text
        history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": reply})
        print(f"Assistant: {reply}\n")

run_chatbot()
The chatbot never calls any tool, never takes any action beyond returning text. Everything it produces is informational. The user decides what to do with it.
Building an agent for the same domain
Adding tool use transforms a chatbot into an agent. The same LLM now makes decisions that result in real-world effects:
This agent reads files, modifies them, and runs tests — all without asking the user for permission at each step. That capability is what distinguishes it from a chatbot.
Examples
Simple chatbot: customer FAQ

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

FAQ_CONTEXT = """
Company: Acme SaaS
Refund policy: 30-day money-back guarantee, no questions asked.
Plans: Starter ($29/mo), Pro ($79/mo), Enterprise (contact sales).
Support email: support@acme.example
Uptime SLA: 99.9% for Pro and Enterprise plans.
"""

def faq_chatbot(question: str, history: list) -> str:
    history.append({"role": "user", "content": question})
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        system=f"You are a customer support agent for Acme SaaS. Answer questions using only this information:\n{FAQ_CONTEXT}\nIf unsure, say so and direct the user to support@acme.example.",
        messages=history
    )
    answer = response.content[0].text
    history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": answer})
    return answer

history = []
print(faq_chatbot("What's your refund policy?", history))
print(faq_chatbot("And do you have an uptime guarantee?", history))
Simple agent: automated report generation
Real-world example

A developer productivity platform ships both a chatbot and an agent interface. The chatbot answers questions about code: "What does this function do?" "How do I use this API?" "What's the idiomatic way to handle errors in Go?" These questions require only text output — the developer reads the answer and acts on it themselves. Single-turn, low cost, high safety.

The agent interface accepts tasks: "Refactor the authentication module to use JWTs instead of session tokens," "Write tests for all the uncovered functions in payment.py," or "Migrate this codebase from Python 2 to Python 3." These tasks require reading many files, making a plan, writing code, running tests, fixing failures, and iterating — actions that span dozens of steps and that a chatbot cannot perform.

The platform exposes both interfaces in the same UI. Short questions go to the chatbot; tasks with a checkbox go to the agent. Both use the same underlying LLM — the difference is entirely in how the application layer structures the interaction and what it does with the output.
Common mistakes
Treating an agent like a chatbot

Users interacting with an agent as if it were a chatbot — sending clarifying follow-up messages mid-task — interrupt the agent's loop and cause confusion. A chatbot-style UX (show a text box, return a text reply) is the wrong interface for an agent. Agents need a task-submission UI with a progress indicator, not a conversation thread.
Adding tools to a chatbot without redesigning the loop

# BAD: chatbot with tools but no loop — ignores tool calls after the first response
def bad_chatbot_with_tools(user_message: str, history: list) -> str:
    history.append({"role": "user", "content": user_message})
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8", max_tokens=1024,
        tools=TOOLS,  # added tools, but no loop to execute them
        messages=history
    )
    # PROBLEM: if stop_reason is "tool_use", we never execute the tool
    # and return an incomplete response
    return response.content[0].text  # may be empty if LLM chose to use a tool

# GOOD: if you add tools, you must add the execution loop
def good_agent(goal: str, history: list) -> str:
    history.append({"role": "user", "content": goal})
    for _ in range(20):
        response = client.messages.create(model="claude-opus-4-8", max_tokens=1024, tools=TOOLS, messages=history)
        if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            return next(b.text for b in response.content if hasattr(b, "text"))
        results = [execute_tool(b) for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use"]
        history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
        history.append({"role": "user", "content": results})
    return "Step limit reached."
Not setting a step limit

An agent loop with no step limit can run indefinitely if the LLM never outputs an end-turn response — a real failure mode when the model gets confused or receives unexpected tool results. Always cap the loop:
Best practices
Match the UX to the pattern
. Chatbots get a conversation UI. Agents get a task submission form with status updates. Mixing the two creates a confusing user experience.
Show agent progress
. Users waiting for an agent need to see what it is doing — log each tool call to the UI. Opacity breeds distrust.
Prefer chatbot for ambiguous requests
. If the user's intent is unclear, use a chatbot to clarify before launching an agent. An agent that acts on a misunderstood goal can cause real damage.
Gate irreversible actions
. Even in an otherwise autonomous agent, require confirmation before actions that cannot be undone (deletion, sending, publishing).
Use different models for different contexts
. A chatbot handling simple questions can use a smaller, faster, cheaper model. An agent orchestrating complex tasks benefits from a larger, more capable model for the reasoning loop.
Log agent runs end-to-end
. Store every tool call, input, and result. This log is essential for debugging, auditing, and improving the agent over time.
Performance & scalability

Chatbot cost scales linearly with conversation length: each turn adds to the context window, increasing the token count of subsequent calls. For long conversations, context management (summarization or truncation) becomes necessary to control costs.
Agent cost is harder to predict. A single agent run may involve N tool calls, each adding to the context. If
is the step count and
is the token count at step
, total tokens are
, which grows quadratically with step count if tool results are large. Monitoring token usage per step and truncating long tool results is important for cost control in production agents.

For throughput, chatbots scale trivially: they are stateless (beyond the session) and can run in parallel without coordination. Agents may have external state side effects — two agents running in parallel on the same filesystem or database can conflict. Design agent systems to be aware of concurrent access.
Tooling & ecosystem

Chatbot frameworks are mature and abundant. The Anthropic SDK, OpenAI SDK, and providers like Bedrock all expose a simple messages API. Frontend frameworks (Vercel AI SDK, LangChain's chat models) add streaming and conversation management.
Agent frameworks add the tool execution loop, observability, and safety controls. Key options:
LangChain AgentExecutor
: the most widely used agent framework; extensive tool library and integrations.
LangGraph
: state-machine-based agent framework for more controlled, inspectable agent flows.
AutoGen (Microsoft)
: multi-agent framework with built-in human-in-the-loop patterns.
Anthropic native tool use
: the messages API with tools is sufficient for most agents without any additional framework.

# Minimal chatbot with streaming (Anthropic SDK)
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain recursion in one paragraph."}]
) as stream:
    for text in stream.text_stream:
        print(text, end="", flush=True)
print()
Comparison with alternatives
Property
Chatbot
Agent
Output type
Text
Effects (files, API calls, messages)
Control flow
User drives each turn
LLM drives each step
LLM calls per task
1 per user turn
N per task (variable)
Tool use
None
Core capability
Self-correction
No
Yes — observes errors and retries
Operational risk
Low (text only)
Higher (real-world effects)
Cost predictability
High (1 call/turn)
Low (N calls, variable)
Best for
Q&A, drafting, explanation
Automation, research, coding tasks
Security & reliability

Chatbots are inherently low-risk in terms of direct system impact. The main security concerns are prompt injection (users trying to jailbreak the system prompt) and output safety (generating harmful or false content). These are well-understood problems with established mitigations.
Agents carry significantly higher security risk because they act. Concrete mitigations:
Principle of least privilege
: give the agent only the tools and permissions it needs for the specific task, not broad system access.
Prompt injection defense
: tool results (web pages, files, API responses) can contain adversarial instructions. Sanitize or isolate external content before including it in the agent's context.
Confirmation gates
: require human approval before irreversible actions. Even a fully autonomous agent should pause before deleting data or sending external communications.
Sandboxed execution
: run code-executing agents in isolated environments (containers, VMs) where filesystem and network access are scoped.
Rate limits on tools
: prevent an agent in a loop from making thousands of API calls or writing gigabytes of data through runaway execution.
Mental model

Think of a chatbot as an expert consultant sitting across the table. You ask a question; they give advice. They might ask clarifying questions. They produce a document or a recommendation. But they don't actually go and do anything — you take their output and act on it yourself.

An agent is more like an employee you can delegate to. You give them a task — "schedule the meeting, book the flights, prepare the agenda" — and they go do it. They have access to your calendar, your email, your booking systems. They check in when they hit a decision they cannot make alone. But between check-ins, they are working autonomously, not waiting for your next message.
Both are valuable. You consult an expert for their opinion; you delegate to an employee for execution. The same relationship governs chatbots and agents.
Key takeaways
Chatbots produce text; agents produce effects — the distinction is in action scope, not model capability.
Chatbots are user-driven (one message → one reply); agents are goal-driven (one goal → autonomous loop until done).
Adding tool use to a chatbot without adding the tool-execution loop is a common bug that silently fails.
Match UX to pattern: conversation UI for chatbots, task-submission UI for agents.
Agents carry higher operational and security risk — apply least-privilege tool access, step limits, and confirmation gates for irreversible actions.
Always impose a hard step limit on agent loops to prevent runaway execution and unbounded cost.
The two patterns are complementary, not competing — production systems commonly use both, routing tasks to the appropriate pattern based on complexity and risk.
